title: Navarin printanier
subtitle: Spring lamb stew with new-season vegetables
excerpt: The spring version of the classic French navarin: young lamb shoulder braised in a light broth and finished with baby turnips, baby carrots, new potatoes, and fresh peas. The vegetables go in towards the end so they keep their shape and colour. Light, fresh, and seasonal.
sourceNotes:
Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), public domain. Navarin printanier as a distinct spring variation of the classic navarin, with the addition of new-season vegetables.

Prosper Montagné, Larousse Gastronomique (1938), public domain edition. The distinction between navarin d'agneau (root vegetable braise) and navarin printanier (spring garnish), and the timing for adding young vegetables.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The lamb braise can be made a day ahead and refrigerated. Skim the fat from the surface, then reheat gently and add the spring vegetables fresh before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: The spring version of navarin d'agneau, the classic French braised lamb stew. Printanier means 'of spring' in French. The dish uses young lamb and is finished with new-season vegetables such as baby turnips, baby carrots, new potatoes, and fresh peas, added towards the end of the cook so they retain their colour.

content.text: is the spring counterpart to the autumn navarin d'agneau: the same braised lamb shoulder, the same clear broth, but finished at the end with baby turnips, baby carrots, new potatoes, and peas instead of the heavier root vegetables. The key difference is timing. The lamb takes 90 minutes; the spring vegetables go in for the last 20 to 30 minutes only, so they stay bright and just tender rather than collapsing into the broth.

Use the youngest vegetables you can find: small turnips no bigger than a golf ball, slim carrots, new potatoes that need only scrubbing rather than peeling. The dish should look and taste of the season it celebrates. If peas are not yet available, broad beans or green beans work equally well.

items.prepNote: baby carrots, scrubbed and halved if large

content.text: A heavy casserole with a tight lid is all the specialist kit you need. The dish cooks first on the hob, then in the oven for the long braise, and comes back to the hob for the spring vegetables at the end.
Heat the oven to 160°C fan. Season the lamb and dust with {{plain-flour}} of plain flour. Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a casserole over high heat and
the meat in two batches until well coloured on all sides, 3 to 4 minutes per batch. Remove and set aside.

Lower the heat to medium. Cook the {{onion}} chopped onion for 5 minutes. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and stir for 1 minute. Add {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and stir for 1 minute more. Pour in {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine and scrape up the browned bits. Add {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock, {{thyme}} of thyme, and {{bay-leaves}} of bay. Return the lamb to the pot. The liquid should reach two-thirds up the meat. Bring to a simmer, cover tightly, and transfer to the oven. Cook for 1 hour 15 minutes.

Return the casserole to the hob over medium heat. Add the {{turnip}} turnips, {{carrot}} carrots, and {{potato}} new potatoes to the broth around the lamb. Bring back to a simmer, cover, and cook on the hob for 20 minutes until the vegetables are just tender. Add the {{peas-frozen}} of peas for the final 3 minutes. Remove the herb sprigs and bay. Taste and adjust the seasoning. Scatter over the chopped parsley and serve straight from the pot.

items.cause: Added too early or cooked at too high a temperature
items.fix: Add the vegetables only in the final 20 minutes, and keep the heat at a gentle simmer. Peas go in for 3 minutes only.

items.cause: Braise cooked at a rolling boil rather than a gentle simmer
items.fix: The flavour is unaffected. Next time, check the pot is barely simmering in the oven and reduce the temperature by 10°C if needed.

items.symptom: Lamb is still chewy at the 1 hour 15 minute mark
items.cause: Older or thicker pieces of shoulder need more time
items.fix: Return to the oven for another 20 to 30 minutes before adding the vegetables.

items.cause: Too much stock or lid not tight enough
items.fix: Remove the lamb and vegetables, then reduce the broth on the hob for 5 minutes. Return everything to warm through.

content.text: The navarin printanier has always been a seasonal marker in French cookery: the dish that signals the end of the root-vegetable months and the arrival of the first tender spring produce. It appears in every French cooking manual from the 19th century onwards as the spring counterpart to the heavier autumn stew, and it is still a feature of French restaurant menus in April and May. The timing is the point: the braise is built slowly, but the spring vegetables arrive late and leave quickly. Serve with crusty bread and a chilled glass of Loire rosé.
